shortDefinition: The deadline to pay your credit card bill, usually 18-21 days after your statement date, to avoid interest and late fees.
definition: The due date is the last day you can pay your credit card bill without being charged interest or a late payment fee. It's set by the bank and typically falls 18 to 21 days after your statement date, giving you a window to review your bill and arrange payment. Paying the full 'total due' amount by this date keeps your account interest-free. Paying only the 'minimum due' avoids a late fee but still triggers interest on the remaining balance. Missing the due date entirely usually means both a late fee and interest charges.
whyItMatters: The due date is the single most important date on your credit card statement. Paying late — even by a day — can cost you a late fee, interest charges, and in some cases a dent to your credit score if the bank reports it. It's the deadline that determines whether using a credit card cost you nothing or cost you real money.
realLifeExamples: Your statement shows ₹12,000 total due with a due date of the 25th. If you pay ₹12,000 by the 25th, you owe nothing extra. If you pay only ₹1,200 (a typical minimum due) or miss the date, the bank starts charging interest on the unpaid balance, plus possibly a late fee.
commonMistakes: Waiting until the due date itself to initiate payment — bank transfers or UPI payments can occasionally take a day to reflect, causing an accidental late payment.
tips: Set up auto-pay for at least the minimum due as a safety net, and still aim to pay the full amount manually before the due date.

faqs.question: What exactly happens if I miss my due date?
faqs.answer: You'll typically be charged a late payment fee plus interest on your outstanding balance, calculated from the transaction dates. Repeated missed payments can also lower your credit score.

faqs.question: Is there any grace period after the due date?
faqs.answer: Generally no — most Indian banks apply late fees and interest starting the day after the due date, though a very small number may offer a day or two of buffer. Don't rely on this.
